parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around the strong asymmetry in private values for BLUE and RED. Buffalo highly values BLUE and dislikes RED, so Buffalo's natural incentive is to give up RED or possibly GREEN in order to acquire BLUE. Bear values both BLUE and RED, with BLUE especially valuable, so Bear is a demanding counterparty: Bear will usually avoid giving away BLUE unless the compensation is very attractive, but Bear is a willing buyer of RED from either other player because Bear values RED much more than they likely do. Goose values GREEN at the common $0.50 and values both RED and BLUE very little, so Goose is the most likely sink for low-value colors and the most willing accepter of trades that convert RED or BLUE into GREEN. Because players only know their own values, proposals must be based on inference from acceptances and inventory changes rather than direct knowledge of who values what. Early-round uncertainty should produce some exploratory offers. Over time, accepted trades should reveal that Goose is comparatively easy to trade with and that Bear is hard to induce to part with BLUE. Buffalo's high reflection and tolerance for unequal outcomes make targeted, self-beneficial offers likely; Bear's mixed cooperative signals but moderate responder standards suggest selective acceptance of clearly profitable trades; Goose's low rejection threshold and low values for RED/BLUE suggest willingness to accept many positive-surplus offers. The main uncertainty is whether proposers risk aggressive offers to test counterparties or instead make modest proposals likely to be accepted. A plausible full-game path is that Buffalo repeatedly tries to turn low-value RED into BLUE, Bear trades away some GREEN to collect RED, and Goose accepts deals that swap low-value RED/BLUE for GREEN or occasionally for small amounts of the other low-value color when profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has revealed information through behavior yet, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative. Buffalo should be the most motivated to seek BLUE using RED because that is Buffalo's clearest private arbitrage. Bear may test whether others will part with RED in exchange for GREEN, since Bear privately values RED above GREEN. Goose, with low private values for RED and BLUE, is likely to use an initial turn to convert one of those low-value colors into GREEN if possible.

By round 1, one useful pattern is visible: Bear successfully bought RED with GREEN, and Goose was willing to trade away BLUE for GREEN. Buffalo now has evidence that Goose may be the easier trading partner and that GREEN can function as a medium of exchange. Bear has learned that others may not readily sell BLUE, so Bear may continue focusing on acquiring RED when profitable. Goose, after gaining RED and losing some BLUE, still has incentive to trade low-value colors for GREEN.

In the final round, trading patterns are more established. Buffalo has likely inferred that direct RED-for-BLUE proposals are hard to land, but Goose remains a plausible counterparty for GREEN-related exchanges. Bear has repeated evidence that Goose is a good source of RED and may continue exploiting that. Goose is likely to make a final value-improving conversion of BLUE into GREEN if inventory allows. End-game incentives also reduce any reason to hold out for future rounds after turn completion, so modest positive-surplus trades become especially plausible.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED most at $1.00, GREEN identically at $0.50, and differ only on BLUE: otter values BLUE at $0.40, peacock at $0.50, and cat at $0.70. That makes BLUE the main source of trade gains. Otter is the natural seller of BLUE and strongest buyer of RED; cat is the natural buyer of BLUE and relatively least attached to RED among the three, though RED still remains highly valuable to everyone. Because RED is worth more than BLUE for every player, straightforward RED-for-BLUE trades are hard to make acceptable to both sides unless quantities are favorable to the RED giver. GREEN is common value and mainly serves as a neutral medium, but since it is worth less than RED to everyone and equal to BLUE only for peacock, it is unlikely to unlock many large-surplus deals. The key uncertainty is private information: players do not know others' exact BLUE values, so early offers are likely to test whether someone is willing to overpay for BLUE or cheaply part with RED. Given the personas, otter likely prefers clear, calculator-positive trades and may make practical but somewhat self-favoring offers; peacock appears tolerant of unequal outcomes and may accept modest gains rather than hold out; cat is also tolerant of unequal splits and relatively prosocial, but with the highest BLUE value should often reject offers that give away BLUE too cheaply and may actively try to acquire more BLUE. Overall, the most likely game path is cautious probing, few accepted trades, and any successful exchange involving cat acquiring BLUE by giving up some GREEN rather than RED.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone begins symmetrically with 10 of each chip, but only their own values are known privately. That usually produces exploratory offers. Otter has the weakest BLUE value and strongest incentive to trade BLUE away for either RED or enough GREEN, while cat has the strongest incentive to accumulate BLUE. Because all players value RED highly, requests for RED are likely to be rejected unless the proposer offers a very favorable quantity in return. Early acceptances therefore most plausibly occur when someone offers BLUE and asks for GREEN, especially if cat is on the receiving side of BLUE.

After round 0, otter likely holds fewer BLUE and more GREEN, while cat holds extra BLUE and fewer GREEN. This sharpens incentives: otter is now even more interested in using surplus GREEN to seek RED, but such proposals remain hard to land because RED is everyone's top-valued chip. Peacock, still near the starting allocation, may continue making low-risk offers, and cat may protect her enlarged BLUE inventory because BLUE is especially valuable to her. The round is therefore likely to feature ambitious but declined attempts to convert GREEN into RED or to buy BLUE too cheaply.

By the final round, players have observed mostly failed attempts and one earlier BLUE-for-GREEN exchange. That history suggests they have learned that RED is too hard to extract and that only clear calculator-positive trades will be accepted. Peacock moves first and may try a modest BLUE purchase with GREEN, but the non-senders can compare that against their own values and will reject if it is not profitable. Otter then may make a final practical offer using BLUE if any remain, but inventory constraints from the earlier accepted trade limit scope. Cat closes the game and, with the highest BLUE value, is unlikely to part with BLUE; instead she may make one last attempt to turn GREEN into BLUE, which is acceptable only if otter still has enough BLUE and sees a positive gain. A conservative forecast is that final-round offers are mostly screened tightly, with no additional trade unless the terms are strongly favorable to the BLUE seller.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should center on red-blue exchange rather than green. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it creates little speculative advantage and mainly serves as a neutral payment chip. By contrast, red and blue have strong cross-player differences: badger values RED and BLUE equally highly at $0.80 each, camel values RED more than BLUE ($0.60 vs $0.40), and squid values BLUE much more than RED ($0.60 vs $0.20). That means camel is the natural demander of RED, squid is the natural demander of BLUE, and badger is relatively flexible because both private colors are highly valuable to him. From an efficiency perspective, RED should tend to move away from squid and toward camel or badger, while BLUE should tend to move away from camel and toward badger or squid. The strategic complication is that players do not know one another's values; they only see whether a posted trade is privately profitable for themselves. So proposals are likely to start somewhat exploratory, with players using simple ratios that are clearly favorable from their own side while hoping at least one counterpart also benefits. The acceptance environment is also noisy because two counterparties can both accept a trade, in which case the final recipient is random. Given the personas, camel and squid seem relatively tolerant responders and less likely to reject profitable but uneven trades, while badger looks somewhat more balanced and adaptive. Overall, the most likely full-game path is a sequence of modest, positive-surplus red-blue trades, with occasional declined offers when a proposer asks for too much of the color they most want.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, nobody has learned much from revealed behavior yet, so proposals are likely to be simple and value-driven. Badger begins with no strong reason to target green, since green is only middling for him and for everyone else. A plausible opening move is to try acquiring BLUE from camel by paying RED one-for-one, because badger values both at $0.80 and camel prefers RED to BLUE. Camel then has a good opportunity to lean into his comparative advantage and request RED while offering BLUE, a trade squid would reject but badger would often like. Squid, seeing the emerging pattern, is likely to ask for BLUE and pay with RED, which should be attractive to badger and unattractive to camel.

After round 0, the public history would suggest that red-blue swaps are feasible and that badger is a common counterparty. Holdings now matter: badger has accumulated extra RED and given up some BLUE, camel has improved his RED position but still values additional RED, and squid has increased BLUE at the cost of RED. Because accepted trades reveal some willingness patterns, proposers may become a bit more ambitious. Badger may try a slightly tougher ask from camel, but if he pushes beyond camel's gain condition the offer should fail. Camel is still incentivized to keep converting BLUE into RED, while squid remains incentivized to convert remaining RED into BLUE when possible.

By the final round, players have observed enough to form rough beliefs about who tends to accept what. End-game behavior often shifts toward squeezing out one last favorable trade rather than experimenting. Badger now knows camel is the most promising source of BLUE and squid rarely values RED enough to pay much for it. Camel should continue targeting RED if inventory allows, but after several trades his BLUE stock may be thinner, limiting scale. Squid, having repeatedly succeeded by offering RED for BLUE, is likely to keep using that template. Because there is no future retaliation beyond this round, proposers may test slightly more aggressive ratios, but responders still see exact personal surplus, so only individually profitable offers should clear.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely driven by strong private-value asymmetries around red and blue chips, with green acting as a stable outside option because it is worth $0.50 to everyone. Dog values RED most highly ($1.00) and BLUE very little ($0.20), so dog should usually try to acquire red or avoid giving it up, while being relatively willing to pay with blue. Butterfly values BLUE most highly ($0.90) and values RED moderately ($0.60), so butterfly is the natural buyer of blue and may also be willing to surrender red at some prices if blue can be gained. Ram values GREEN most, then RED at $0.40, and BLUE least at $0.10, so ram is the clearest seller of blue and likely prefers green over most other assets. Because each player only knows their own values, proposals must be based on guesses about what others might accept. The acceptance interface makes each responder's own gain or loss transparent, so accepted trades should usually be individually profitable for the realized recipient. The main uncertainty is therefore not whether a clearly profitable responder accepts, but whether proposers can infer enough about counterparties' hidden values to make acceptable offers. Persona priors suggest all three players are fairly tolerant of unequal outcomes and not especially prone to rejecting merely for fairness reasons, so behavior should skew pragmatic rather than punitive. A likely pattern is early exploratory offers using green as a safe medium, then some adjustment once public trade history reveals who seems eager to move which colors. Dog is the player with the largest potential arbitrage if able to trade away blue for red, but others may not give up red cheaply. Butterfly is strategically positioned to convert red or green into blue. Ram may make simpler, conservative offers that improve holdings with low downside, especially exchanging away blue for green or possibly red if the quantity ratio is favorable.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no revealed history beyond identical starting endowments, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative. Dog should begin by testing whether anyone will part with red in exchange for blue, since that is dog's biggest private-value opportunity, but dog may avoid too extreme a request because uncertainty is still high. Butterfly likely searches for blue, the chip he values most, and may use green or red to pay. Ram is likely to prefer straightforward, safe trades that convert low-value blue into green.

By round 1, one public fact has emerged: ram was willing to take green in exchange for blue, and dog was willing to take blue in exchange for green at the proposed ratio. Butterfly has also successfully acquired blue, which may signal to others that blue is not equally valuable to everyone. Dog may now infer that red remains hard to obtain directly and might try a greener, more acceptable offer. Butterfly, now holding extra blue, may pivot toward acquiring red or replenishing green. Ram may continue to exploit blue disposal opportunities but with somewhat better terms if possible.

In the final round, players no longer need to preserve future bargaining reputation much, so proposals can become more targeted and surplus-seeking. Dog has managed to add some red and may still want more, but final-round feasibility depends on what others now hold. Butterfly has learned that direct red-for-blue requests are not being accepted and may instead use green to buy more blue if possible. Ram, after repeatedly moving blue, is likely to continue conservative value-improving exchanges if available, but may also face diminished inventory flexibility.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely driven by a clear double-coincidence structure in private values. alligator-blue-3197 values BLUE most ($0.60) and RED least ($0.30), lion-orange-6194 values BLUE most strongly of all ($1.00) and is relatively lukewarm on RED ($0.40), while iguana-orange-3232 values RED most ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.40). GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so it works as a relatively safe medium but creates no strict surplus by itself unless exchanged against a player's low-value color. The biggest potential gains come from moving RED away from alligator to iguana or moving BLUE away from iguana to either alligator or lion. However, players do not know others' exact values, so they must infer from acceptance behavior and public holdings. Because responders can see their own exact surplus from any posted offer, trades with negative private value should usually be declined. That creates pressure for proposals to be individually rational for some recipient, but competition is noisy because a sender cannot choose among multiple acceptors. Persona priors suggest low rejection propensity across players, especially lion-orange-6194 and iguana-orange-3232, so if a proposal is privately profitable they are likely to accept rather than hold out on fairness grounds. alligator-blue-3197 is likely to seek structured, favorable trades and may start cautiously by offering low-value RED for BLUE or GREEN. lion-orange-6194 has the strongest hunger for BLUE and may resist giving it up, instead trying to acquire RED cheaply using GREEN. iguana-orange-3232 strongly values RED and may be willing to give up BLUE for it. Overall, the forecast is for at least one early alligator-iguana trade swapping RED for BLUE, followed by more difficult bargaining because lion's most valuable asset is BLUE, which lion is unlikely to sell at terms others can profitably accept. Later rounds therefore likely feature increasingly targeted but often declined proposals as inventories change and players learn from earlier acceptances.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip. The most salient efficient direction is for alligator-blue-3197 to trade away RED, which she values least, in exchange for BLUE, which she values most. iguana-orange-3232 is the natural counterparty because she values RED highly and BLUE relatively little. lion-orange-6194 also likes BLUE very much and should be reluctant to offer it away. Because no one yet has revealed much through behavior, opening proposals are likely to test whether obvious gains from trade can be captured.

After round 0, alligator-blue-3197 has more BLUE and fewer RED, while iguana-orange-3232 has more RED and less BLUE. The successful opening trade publicly signals that someone found alligator's RED-for-BLUE terms attractive, which may make future RED requests from iguana plausible. lion-orange-6194 has learned less directly but still faces the basic constraint that giving away BLUE is expensive for her. Middle-round bargaining is likely to feature more selective proposals shaped by updated inventories: alligator still wants BLUE but now has less RED to spend, lion may continue probing for cheap RED, and iguana may seek additional RED if terms are favorable.

By the final round, the easiest surplus trades have mostly been attempted. alligator-blue-3197 has converted some low-value RED into higher-value assets, lion-orange-6194 has picked up some RED without sacrificing BLUE, and iguana-orange-3232 has already improved her RED position from the first-round trade. Endgame incentives now favor sharper, more self-serving offers because there is no future relationship within this game. Yet the acceptance rule still disciplines proposers: each receiver sees exact private surplus and can simply decline bad offers. This makes late-round attempts that demand BLUE from lion or extra RED from alligator harder to complete unless terms are clearly favorable to the recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players begin with symmetric inventories but asymmetric values. Since nobody knows others' private values, proposals are likely to probe broadly plausible exchange rates rather than perfectly target a counterpart. Turtle has the strongest incentive to seek blue and is likely to test whether someone will surrender blue for green. Gorilla has the strongest incentive to seek red and may hope zebra or turtle will sell red cheaply for green. Zebra, being relatively low-value on both private-value colors and more prosocial in prior tasks, may make a simple trade that is clearly profitable to at least one counterpart.

By round 1, trade history reveals some directional information. Gorilla successfully bought red with green, suggesting someone was willing to part with red; zebra then sold blue for green, and turtle accepted, revealing turtle's appetite for blue even at a cost in green. Holdings now matter: turtle has extra blue and less green, gorilla has extra red and less green, zebra has more green and less of the private-value colors. This makes zebra even more likely to keep serving as a counterparty, while turtle may now test buying red or replenishing green only at favorable rates. Gorilla may exploit zebra's inventory again if zebra still holds enough red or blue.

In the final round, there is no future strategic benefit from experimentation, so proposals should focus on immediate positive surplus given current holdings. Turtle now likely has accumulated some red and still values both red and blue highly, so turtle may try one last green-for-blue style bid if inventory permits. Gorilla, now with stronger red inventory and diminished need for more, may try to trade a red chip for multiple greens or a blue chip only if personally profitable. Zebra, with comparatively low values and a cooperative prior, may make a simple liquidating trade using green to acquire something only if the arithmetic is favorable, but zebra may also fail to find an accepted deal if asking too much. Final-round behavior should therefore feature sharper acceptance thresholds and fewer speculative offers.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone has 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints do not yet bind. With no trade history, proposers face maximum uncertainty about others' values and are likely to begin with simple, salient exchanges. Cat has the clearest profitable direction from the analyst view: offer BLUE to get RED. Zebra should privately like that, while iguana should dislike giving up RED unless the compensation is generous. After seeing cat's offer and responses, iguana may infer that BLUE can be used as a bargaining chip, but may still try a cautious offer that keeps downside low. Zebra then has good reason to propose RED for BLUE as well, targeting the same comparative-advantage pattern from the opposite side.

By now the public history suggests that RED-for-BLUE trades are feasible and that cat and zebra are especially natural counterparts. Holdings have also shifted: cat now has extra RED and fewer BLUE, while zebra has extra BLUE and fewer RED. That reduces but does not eliminate the incentive for more exchange between them. Iguana has observed two accepted RED-for-BLUE deals and may rationally test whether offering BLUE can obtain RED, but because iguana values RED strongly, acceptable rates are limited. GREEN may now appear in proposals as a safer balancing asset because it has common value and creates less uncertainty for recipients.

In the final round there is no future strategic value to experimentation, so proposals are more likely to be narrowly myopic: each sender should post the best deal they think someone might still accept. Cat still benefits from a final small BLUE-for-RED exchange if zebra has enough RED left and wants more BLUE. Iguana, having failed with earlier attempts, may simplify and try converting BLUE into GREEN, which is easier for others to evaluate but still may not clear if priced too aggressively. Zebra closes with one more attempt to leverage low-value RED for high-value BLUE, again most plausibly against cat. Because acceptances are private and only one recipient is realized if multiple accept, some offers may be designed to appeal to either non-sender, but the strongest bilateral fit remains cat-zebra.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has relatively weak scope for large mutually beneficial trade because all three players value GREEN identically at $0.50, while RED and BLUE differ only modestly across players. The clearest value asymmetries are that swan-purple-1670 values RED less ($0.50) than bear-purple-7271 and peacock-red-7768 ($0.80), and peacock-red-7768 values BLUE slightly less ($0.70) than the two purple players ($0.80). Since players only know their own values during play, they cannot directly target the highest-value trading partner; they must post public offers that any non-sender can accept, with random matching if both accept. That creates uncertainty and discourages finely tuned offers. A rational sender using the on-screen calculator will only want trades that are at least weakly beneficial to themselves, while recipients will accept only when their own calculator shows a gain. Because bear-purple-7271 values RED and BLUE equally, bear is likely to be comparatively flexible about swapping those colors but unwilling to overpay in GREEN. Peacock-red-7768 is slightly BLUE-light and may try to convert BLUE into RED or hold RED tightly, while swan-purple-1670 has the strongest motive to give up RED in exchange for BLUE or GREEN. Persona priors suggest all three are fairly tolerant of unequal deals and not especially prone to rejecting on fairness grounds, so acceptance should mostly track private surplus rather than norms. The likely pattern is some early probing offers, several declines when proposals fail for one recipient or are too ambitious, and at least one accepted trade involving swan giving RED or peacock giving BLUE, where the proposer can secure a small sure gain without needing strong trust.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, all players start symmetric in inventory, so proposals are driven entirely by private values and beliefs about what others might accept. Because nobody has observed acceptance behavior yet, first offers are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative. Bear should recognize that swapping RED for BLUE is value-neutral for herself and may test whether someone wants RED enough to part with BLUE. Peacock has a mild incentive to shed BLUE or acquire RED, and swan is the most plausible source of RED because RED is comparatively low value to her.

By round 1, the public history suggests that RED-for-BLUE swaps can clear when directed implicitly toward the right recipient. Bear now holds extra RED and fewer BLUE, while swan holds extra BLUE and fewer RED. That makes bear somewhat more willing to swap back toward BLUE if terms are favorable, and it gives peacock information that both others may react differently to color-specific offers. In the final round, strategic experimentation matters less because there is no future opportunity. Players should make any remaining positive-surplus trade they think can clear. Bear and swan have repeatedly found compatible RED-for-BLUE exchange, so that channel remains the most likely. Peacock still has only a small valuation wedge and may continue to struggle to post a trade that is profitable for himself and also acceptable to someone else. Final-round offers may therefore include one more accepted bear-swan swap and another peacock attempt that is too demanding to attract acceptance.

Camel then has incentives to convert green into red or blue, but without knowing others' values may post a conservative red-seeking offer. But since players do not know others' valuations, proposals will be shaped by inference from acceptance behavior and by the UI showing exact own-payoff consequences. A likely dynamic is that alligator, being relatively prosocial and tolerant, experiments with a moderate offer rather than an extreme extraction attempt; otter, who is rejection-tolerant and self-protective rather than generous, is likely to make efficient self-benefiting offers that still leave the responder weakly better off; tiger, with a somewhat prosocial style but positive own valuation for RED, is unlikely to give away RED cheaply and may instead try for BLUE using GREEN or ask for RED on terms others decline. Uncertainty comes from partner competition: if a posted trade is profitable for both non-senders, either may accept, and if both accept the recipient is random. Because all players start with abundant inventories and there are only three rounds, the game likely features a few simple, calculator-positive exchanges and several declined ambitious proposals.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, nobody has behavioral evidence about others' values, so first-round offers are likely to probe broadly for willingness to trade. Alligator has the clearest incentive to try converting BLUE into RED, but a one-for-one blue-for-red request would only be accepted by otter, not by tiger, and alligator may shade the offer upward to improve acceptance chances. Otter has the best objective trading position in the game because otter values BLUE highly and RED weakly, so offering BLUE for RED can create gains for both sides, especially with alligator. Tiger is less naturally positioned for efficient trade because tiger also likes RED, so tiger is more likely to test a speculative or neutral-looking green-based proposal.

After round 0, players can infer some structure from revealed acceptances. Alligator and otter have each seen the other accept red-blue swaps, suggesting that these two are natural trading counterparts. Tiger has also just learned that asking others to give up RED for only GREEN is unlikely to work. This makes second-round bargaining more targeted: alligator may now push for a slightly better red acquisition, otter may continue exploiting mutually beneficial blue-for-red exchange, and tiger may shift toward trying to obtain BLUE with RED or GREEN, though tiger still faces the problem that BLUE is not scarce enough for others to value highly.

By the final round, trade patterns are clearer. Alligator has repeatedly succeeded in getting RED from otter by paying BLUE, while otter has confirmed that tiger will part with RED for enough BLUE only if the terms are not too favorable to the requester. With only one proposal each remaining, players have less reason to experiment and more reason to post calculator-positive offers that have a real chance of acceptance. Still, final-round ambition can increase because there is no future reputational cost, so one or more players may make a slightly tougher offer that gets declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong two-sided gains-from-trade structure centered on red and blue chips. Dolphin values BLUE most ($1.00) and RED very little ($0.20), while Frog values RED most ($1.00) and BLUE little ($0.30). That creates a classic mutually beneficial exchange opportunity: Dolphin should want to give away RED to get BLUE, and Frog should want to give away BLUE to get RED. Pig is comparatively moderate, valuing RED and BLUE both at $0.40, so Pig is less likely to initiate the most efficient trades but may act as an opportunistic acceptor when an offer is weakly favorable. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so green-for-green-equivalent trades are mainly useful as a medium of exchange, but because everyone values green identically it is harder to create strict private gains using GREEN unless it is exchanged against a color someone privately undervalues. Strategically, each proposer sees their own calculator and will usually avoid offers that lose money for themselves if accepted. Responders also know exactly whether acceptance helps them, so accepted trades should usually be individually profitable for both sides. The main uncertainty is not valuation logic but bargaining psychology: players do not know others' exact values, only public ranges and observed behavior. Early proposals may therefore be somewhat exploratory, with Dolphin and Frog especially testing whether the other side will accept favorable red-blue swaps. Persona priors suggest Dolphin is relatively structured and cooperative, likely to make clear positive-sum offers; Frog is generous and acceptance-tolerant, likely to accept beneficial deals even if uneven; Pig has very low rejection tendencies and may accept modestly profitable trades, but is less naturally positioned for large gains. Overall, the most likely path is that Dolphin and Frog complete one or more red-blue trades over the three rounds, while Pig either fails to find a strongly attractive bilateral deal or picks up at most one moderate trade involving GREEN or a small spread in RED/BLUE.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning from behavior. Dolphin is the clearest candidate to open with a red-for-blue proposal because that is strongly beneficial if accepted and easy to justify from Dolphin's own calculator. Frog, who highly values red, is the most likely acceptor of such an offer. Frog's own later proposal is likely to mirror this logic from the other side. Pig begins with no special comparative advantage, so Pig may either offer a cautious trade using GREEN as a neutral asset or propose a modest red-for-blue swap that only one counterpart would find attractive.

By round 1, the revealed history should make Dolphin and Frog more confident that they are natural trading partners. Both have already seen acceptance on red-blue exchanges, which reduces uncertainty and encourages another profitable swap. Pig now also sees that direct gains with either counterpart are limited unless Pig offers terms generous enough to compensate the other side's private opportunity cost. Because Pig's values for RED and BLUE are only $0.40 each, Pig may struggle to beat the Dolphin-Frog channel on surplus.

In the final round, there is no future strategic penalty for pushing slightly harder, but accepted trades still need to be profitable for responders. Dolphin should still seek more BLUE while unloading low-value RED, though shrinking inventories may limit size. Frog still wants RED and can continue paying with BLUE when favorable. Pig may end the game with either a conservative attempt or no deal if remaining inventories and private gains do not align well. Because everyone observes current holdings, later proposals are constrained by what each player still possesses after earlier trades.

Iguana has the strongest incentive to convert RED into BLUE, but may initially ask for a favorable rate. Owl most wants RED and should be willing to part with BLUE. Squid also dislikes RED and may accept offers that take RED away in exchange for BLUE only if the rate is favorable enough. By round 1, the public history reveals that RED-for-BLUE trades can clear and that owl is a likely demander of RED. In the final round, there is no future strategic reputation beyond immediate execution, so proposals should focus on any remaining individually profitable trades. The revealed pattern now strongly points to owl as the high-red player and iguana as the low-red seller. Since owl's BLUE inventory has been reduced by prior accepted trades, rates may tighten.
