parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear value asymmetry: dog-green-8079 and turtle-red-9966 both value BLUE at $1.00 and value RED and GREEN at $0.50, while parrot-pink-5400 values RED slightly more ($0.60), GREEN at $0.50, and BLUE much less ($0.30). That creates a natural trading direction in which dog and turtle want to acquire BLUE and are relatively willing to give up RED, whereas parrot is the only player for whom giving up BLUE can be attractive if enough RED is received in return. Because green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, GREEN works as a neutral currency and is less likely to generate strong gains unless used to fine-tune a deal. The main strategic uncertainty is informational: during play, dog and turtle do not know that the other also values BLUE highly, and parrot does not know exactly how much others value BLUE, only whether posted deals are privately profitable to them. Since recipients have a calculator showing their own net gain, strictly loss-making offers are likely to be rejected. Dog's persona suggests tolerance for unequal outcomes and willingness to make pragmatic offers; parrot's persona suggests somewhat prosocial but uncertainty-averse behavior, so he may prefer simpler, clearly beneficial trades; turtle's persona suggests caution and low openness to trusting speculative offers. Overall, the likely pattern is that early probing offers may fail if they ask too much, then acceptable bilateral trades emerge around parrot exchanging BLUE away for RED, with dog and turtle competing to be counterparties whenever a posted offer benefits them.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. The biggest latent surplus comes from moving BLUE from parrot to either dog or turtle in exchange for RED. But because players only know their own values, the first round likely includes some probing and conservative offers. Dog, as first mover and someone who highly values BLUE, is likely to test whether someone will part with BLUE for a modest amount of RED. Parrot, seeing low value in BLUE and somewhat higher value in RED, is then well positioned to post a clearly profitable exchange. Turtle, who also highly values BLUE, may try a similar swap once the basic market direction is revealed.

After round 0, dog has gained BLUE and lost some RED, parrot has shifted some BLUE into RED, and turtle has also acquired BLUE. Public history now makes it more apparent that RED-for-BLUE trades are getting accepted, though players still do not observe private values directly. Because parrot accepted turtle's offer and had both players accept his own earlier offer, dog and turtle can infer that BLUE is tradable from parrot at the right rate. This round likely features more targeted attempts to exploit that channel, but parrot may become more selective if his BLUE stock falls or if proposals look too one-sided.

By the final round, the broad bargaining pattern is public: parrot is the natural source of BLUE, and dog and turtle are willing buyers using RED. Endgame incentives can create slightly more aggressive asks because there is no future reputational cost. Dog may push for one last favorable trade; parrot may still be happy to monetize low-value BLUE if the price in RED is good enough; turtle may also continue the same strategy but could face reduced willingness from parrot if BLUE inventory is no longer abundant. Since all acceptance decisions remain calculator-based, profitable offers should still go through while marginal or zero-gain offers are likely to fail.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong structural asymmetry. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, blue chips are worth only $0.10 to everyone, and red chips are worth $0.30 to badger-green-4722 and peacock-purple-5231 but $0.50 to unicorn-blue-7096. Because everyone starts with the same inventory, the main efficient direction is for unicorn-blue-7096 to acquire red from the others by paying with green, while badger-green-4722 and peacock-purple-5231 are relatively happy to give up red if they receive enough green. Blue is likely to be close to dead weight in bargaining because no one values it much, so offers involving blue are often unattractive unless quantities are heavily skewed. The uncertainty comes from private information: badger and peacock do not know that unicorn values red more highly, and unicorn does not know that the other two share the same lower red value. That means early proposals may use simple common-value logic or rough heuristics rather than fully efficient trade. Persona priors suggest badger may prefer straightforward, low-conflict offers and may accept modest gains; peacock is more reflective and prosocial, so likely to search for a trade acceptable to someone else; unicorn appears tolerant of unequal splits and not strongly fairness-enforcing, so he is likely to accept profitable offers and may make hard-bargaining proposals when in the proposer role. Overall, the most likely path is that badger and peacock try to turn some low-value red into green, unicorn selectively accepts those offers because red is especially valuable to him, and unicorn then struggles to get others to part with more red unless he offers enough green to beat their own $0.30 red valuation.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 red, 10 blue, and 10 green. Nobody has inventory pressure yet, so opening proposals are likely to test the market. Since green is publicly known to be valuable and blue is publicly weak, early serious proposals should center on red-for-green exchange. Badger and peacock each privately value red less than green, so they have incentive to sell some red for green. Unicorn privately values red and green equally, so he can profit from these trades only when the green paid is less than the red received in his own valuation.

After round 0, badger and peacock each have slightly more green and less red, while unicorn has accumulated extra red and spent some green. Publicly, everyone can see unicorn accepted red-for-green deals twice, which may signal that he is a willing counterparty for more such trades, even if the others cannot infer exactly why. Badger and peacock now have reason to keep exploiting that apparent demand, but unicorn's reduced green inventory makes his own proposal more constrained and may push him toward stingier offers that are less likely to be accepted.

By the final round, the broad pattern is visible: unicorn has become red-heavy, while badger and peacock have improved their holdings by converting some red into green. Final-round incentives reduce the value of experimentation. Badger and peacock are likely to make one more safe positive-surplus offer if inventory allows, while unicorn may still try to acquire red cheaply, but with no future rounds left the others have little reason to accept a marginal deal that does not clearly improve their own payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong asymmetry in private values for RED and BLUE. Dog values BLUE most highly at $1.00 and also likes RED at $0.80. Mouse values BLUE moderately at $0.60 but values RED very little at $0.20. Owl values BLUE highly at $1.00 and RED low at $0.30. GREEN is worth the same to everyone at $0.50, so it functions as a neutral exchange medium. The main efficient direction is therefore RED flowing from mouse or owl toward dog, and BLUE flowing from mouse toward dog or owl is hard because mouse does not own extra BLUE relative to others. Because each player only knows their own values during play, they cannot directly target counterpart-specific gains; instead they must post offers that are clearly acceptable on the recipient side based on whatever private values that recipient happens to have. This creates uncertainty: a proposer may offer a trade that is good for them but unattractive to both listeners. Dog is likely to be the most active and successful trader because dog can profit by giving away GREEN, which is only mid-valued to dog, in order to acquire RED from players who may personally undervalue it. Mouse has the weakest outside options because mouse's cheap chip is RED, but mouse can only offer chips currently held, and others may not want the chips mouse gives up unless the terms are generous. Owl also prefers BLUE and is relatively flexible, but owl does not have an obvious private-information advantage in making offers that others will surely accept. The personas suggest dog and mouse are relatively cooperative and tolerant of unequal splits, so they may accept modest positive-surplus deals rather than insist on hard bargaining. Owl appears adaptive and not especially rejection-prone, so owl may also accept trades that are individually profitable. Overall, the likely pattern is early exploratory offers, at least one successful GREEN-for-RED trade benefiting dog, and then fewer additional deals once inventories shift and the easiest gains are exhausted.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each chip, so no inventory constraint yet matters. Dog has the clearest profitable move: offer GREEN for RED, since dog values RED more than GREEN and either mouse or owl may value RED less than GREEN. Mouse and owl do not know dog's values, but each evaluates only personal payoff, so acceptance depends on whether the posted trade gives them more value than they give up. Round 0 is likely to feature relatively simple probing offers with quantities chosen to make acceptance likely.

After round 0, dog has already improved by converting GREEN into RED through mouse, and mouse now has fewer RED and more GREEN. This makes a second identical dog offer less attractive to mouse because mouse's remaining RED stock is lower, but owl still has full RED inventory. Dog may try again with similar logic, hoping owl accepts. Mouse is now in a tougher trading position because mouse's best chip to part with, RED, is exactly the chip dog values highly, but mouse may ask too much for BLUE and get rejected. Owl may either seek a mild inventory rebalance or post a speculative offer, but owl's offers are less likely to clear unless they visibly compensate the recipient.

By the final round, dog has accumulated extra RED and reduced GREEN, while mouse and owl each have seen that BLUE is hard to extract from others because dog and owl both value BLUE highly. The remaining bargaining space is narrower. Dog may still look for one more favorable GREEN-for-RED exchange, but the easiest counterparties have already traded once and may have less appetite or inventory slack. Final-round proposals are therefore likely to be more ambitious and more often declined, especially when they ask high-value BLUE from players who strongly prefer to keep it.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around red chips, because private values create a strong but uneven red ranking: rabbit values RED at $1.00, alligator at $0.60, and buffalo at $0.50. BLUE also differs, with alligator valuing BLUE most at $0.50, rabbit at $0.30, and buffalo only at $0.20. GREEN is fixed at $0.50 for everyone, so it serves as a common-value settlement chip. The main efficient direction is that rabbit wants to acquire RED, especially from buffalo, who values RED relatively less. Alligator also likes RED somewhat, but not nearly as much as rabbit, and alligator values BLUE highly, so alligator is less eager to give up BLUE cheaply. Because proposals are broadcast to both potential recipients and either accepter can be randomly selected if multiple players accept, proposers have to think not only about whether a trade is profitable for someone, but whether the wrong responder might accept and still leave the proposer okay with the outcome. Since players only know their own values in play, they must infer others from acceptance behavior. Early rounds are therefore likely to feature cautious probing offers near common-value logic, especially involving GREEN as a safe payment chip. Rabbit and buffalo have more prosocial/cooperative priors, so they may make clearer positive-surplus offers rather than extreme take-it-or-leave-it demands. Alligator's prior suggests more self-protective bargaining and tolerance for unequal splits, so his offers are more likely to seek favorable terms and risk rejection. Overall, the likely path is: exploratory or slightly ambitious opening proposals, then at least one buffalo-rabbit trade involving RED for GREEN or BLUE, and some later adjustment once holdings and revealed willingness clarify who is willing to move RED.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, everyone starts symmetric in inventories but asymmetric in values. With no acceptance history yet, proposers are likely to test the market. Alligator has the strongest incentive to hold BLUE and some incentive to gain RED, so an initial ask for RED while paying GREEN is plausible but may be too stingy for rabbit and only borderline for buffalo. Buffalo, being more cooperative and valuing RED and GREEN equally but BLUE poorly, is a natural candidate to offer BLUE or GREEN to obtain something she suspects others may part with. Rabbit strongly values RED and should already look for chances to buy it, especially from buffalo, while trying not to overpay with GREEN.

By round 1, some information has been revealed through acceptance behavior. Buffalo has shown willingness to acquire RED for BLUE from alligator, and buffalo has also shown willingness to sell RED for enough GREEN to rabbit. Rabbit now has evidence that buffalo is the easier source of RED. Alligator has learned that others may not release RED at even trade with GREEN, and also that BLUE can be used advantageously if the price is right. Holdings now matter: buffalo has fewer RED and more GREEN, rabbit has more RED and less GREEN, and alligator has extra BLUE and fewer RED. This should push alligator toward trying to recover RED or unload BLUE selectively, buffalo toward balanced-value trades, and rabbit toward another RED acquisition if terms remain favorable.

In the final round, strategic delay no longer has option value, so proposals can become more direct. Rabbit should still want RED if buffalo has any left, but buffalo may now recognize that RED tends to be demanded and may ask a slightly better price. Alligator, seeing little success in buying RED cheaply, may switch to a safer trade that improves his own mix using BLUE or GREEN. Since everyone can condition on current holdings and there is no future punishment beyond this round, acceptances should track immediate private surplus closely. Final-round behavior is therefore likely to include one more mutually beneficial value-improving exchange and possibly one rejected proposal that asks for too much.
